div.p: Hardware View: Single 32-bit value accessed via bit masks Software View: Structure with named boolean fields for clarity Translation: Compiler packs struct to 4 bytes (sizeof(pte_t) == 4)

text.tspan: // Mark page as accessed (called on every memory access)
// Mark page as dirty (called on write)
// Compose physical address from PTE and offset

g.text: static inline uint32_t compose_pa(uint32_t pte, uint32_t offset) {

text.tspan: // Mark page as accessed (called on every memory access)
// Mark page as dirty (called on write)
// Compose physical address from PTE and offset

div.p: A and D are set by hardware, cleared by OS P, R/W, U/S are set/cleared by OS only
20 bits Physical frame number - specifies which 4KB frame in physical memory
Meaning: Page is present in physical memory 0 = Page not in memory → triggers page fault 1 = Page valid, translation can proceed
Meaning: Read/Write permission 0 = Read-only access (writes trigger protection fault) 1 = Read-write access allowed
Meaning: User/Supervisor privilege level 0 = Supervisor only (kernel mode, ring 0) 1 = User accessible (user mode, ring 3)
Meaning: Page has been accessed (read OR write) Set by hardware on any access to this page Used by OS for LRU/Clock page replacement
Meaning: Page has been written to (modified) Set by hardware on write to this page Used by OS to decide if swap write needed on eviction

text.tspan: // Clear: pte &= ~PTE_DIRTY // OS resets after writeback
// Clear: pte &= ~PTE_DIRTY // OS resets after writeback

div.p: Meaning: Physical Frame Number (bits 31-12) Specifies which 4KB frame in physical memory Combined with 12-bit offset to form physical address
